prompt:
Lunch-league battleship at the office: two people netcat into a referee and play. I need the referee — one file, `battleduel.rb`, Ruby stdlib only (TCPServer/Socket/Thread are all fair game). The acceptance harness `test_battleduel.rb` spawns your server and drives it with two scripted clients; it and the `fixtures/` files are off limits.

Startup: bind 127.0.0.1 on an EPHEMERAL port (the harness runs suites in parallel, never hardcode one) and print exactly one line `PORT <n>` to stdout, immediately flushed. One process referees exactly ONE duel and exits 0 once it's decided — win or forfeit.

Everything on the wire is newline-terminated lines; tolerate a trailing \r on input. First connection is seated as P1 and greeted `HELLO P1`, second as P2 with `HELLO P2`; any further connection gets `ERR full` and is closed. Error replies go ONLY to the offender and never change state.

The board is 8x8: coordinates are a row letter A-H plus a column digit 1-8, like `C5`. The fleet, one of each: BATTLESHIP (4 cells), CRUISER (3), SUBMARINE (3), DESTROYER (2). Placement phase has no turn order — each player independently sends `PLACE <ship> <coord> <H|V>` (coord is the top/left cell; H extends right, V extends down) and gets back `OK PLACE <ship>`. Rejections, checked in this order: wrong number of tokens or an unknown command anywhere -> `ERR bad-command`; after you've readied, any PLACE -> `ERR already-ready`; unknown ship name -> `ERR bad-ship`; that ship already down -> `ERR already-placed`; malformed coordinate -> `ERR bad-coord`; orientation not H or V -> `ERR bad-orient`; ship would leave the board -> `ERR off-board`; ship would sit on a cell one of your placed ships already occupies -> `ERR overlap` (mere adjacency is fine).

`READY` with the fleet incomplete -> `ERR fleet-incomplete`; a second READY -> `ERR already-ready`; otherwise reply `OK READY`, and the moment BOTH players are ready broadcast `START` then `TURN P1` to both.

Battle: `FIRE <coord>` — before START it's `ERR not-started`, out of turn `ERR not-your-turn`, malformed coord `ERR bad-coord`, a cell that shooter has already fired at `ERR repeat-shot` (each shooter has their own shot history). A legal shot is broadcast to BOTH players as `SHOT <shooter> <coord> MISS` / `SHOT <shooter> <coord> HIT` / `SHOT <shooter> <coord> SUNK <ship>` (SUNK announces the ship when its last cell is hit). House rule: you shoot again while you're hitting — after HIT or SUNK broadcast `TURN <same player>`, after MISS `TURN <other player>`. When a shot sinks the last remaining ship, broadcast `WIN <shooter>` instead of a TURN line, close both connections, exit 0.

Forfeits: if a seated player's connection drops any time before the duel is decided, the other seated player (if present) is told `WIN <them> forfeit`, connections close, exit 0.

Mind your threads/IO: the harness interleaves two live sockets, so per-client reads must not block the other player, and broadcasts must reach each client in event order. `ruby test_battleduel.rb` is the gate — the full-game test plays the fixture fleets and shot lists to a P1 win and pins every broadcast.
